en.extract:
The smoke-colored pewee (Contopus fumigatus) is a species of bird in the family Tyrannidae. The species is characterized by a uniform dusky-grey plumage.
Smoke-colored pewees are found in Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, Guyana, Peru, and Venezuela. Their natural habitats are subtropical or tropical dry forests, subtropical or tropical moist montane forests, and heavily degraded former forests.


Taxonomy and systematics
The smoke-colored pewee belongs to the family Tyrannidae, known as the tyrant flycatchers. The Tyrannidae are considered to be the largest family of birds on earth, containing over 400 species. The group shows large variation in form and plumage and can be found in every country in the Americas.
The species was first formally described by the French naturalists Alcide d'Orbigny and Frédéric de Lafresnaye in 1837, based on a specimen found in the Bolivian Yungas. The generic name Contopus derives from the Ancient Greek kontos (pole) and pous (foot). The specific name fumigatus comes from the Latin fumus (smoke).
Six subspecies are recognized:

C. f. ardosiacus (Lafresnaye, 1844) - Colombia and W Venezuela to E Ecuador and NE Peru
C. f. cineraceus (Lafresnaye, 1848) - Subtropical N Venezuela (Yaracuy to Miranda)
C. f. duidae (Chapman, 1929) - Tepuis of S Venezuela (s Bolívar and Amazonas), adj. Guyana
C. f. zarumae (Chapman, 1924) - SW Colombia (Nariño), W Ecuador, and NW Peru (south to southwestern Cajamarca)
C. f. fumigatus - the nominate subspecies (d'Orbigny & Lafresnaye, 1837) - SE Peru (Puno) and W Bolivia (La Paz and Cochabamba)
C. f. brachyrhynchus (Cabanis, 1883) SE Peru (Puno) and W Bolivia (La Paz and Cochabamba)


Description
The smoke-colored pewee is a grey bird with a slight crest on its head. Their beaks are orange with a black culmen. Mature birds are 16–17 cm in size and 18-20g in weight. This species displays no sexual dimorphism, meaning both sexes share the same plumage. Their feet are anisodactyl, therefore three digits on their foot are forward and one is back. This is the most common foot type in perching birds.


Plumages
Adult smoke-colored pewees have a uniform grey plumage, though the upper body and wing tips may be tinged with olive or a pale brown. The crown is a dark gray with a crest. The edges of the tertials may be a light brown. Two pale, narrow wingbars may be present, but near indistinct. The throat, breast, and belly may be a slightly paler grey. The undertail-coverts are yellowish-white. Both sexes look alike. As you move North in smoke-colored pewee habitat range, birds typically become darker and greyer.
Juvenile smoke-colored pewees are typically lighter and browner than mature birds. A fringed buff of feathers can be seen at the bird's nape. Two to three wingbars are visible, and both the wingbars and belly are slightly ochre.


Habitat and distribution
The smoke-colored pewee is endemic to the countries of Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, Guyana, Peru, and Venezuela. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical dry forests, subtropical or tropical moist montane forests, and heavily degraded former forests. Birds are typically found at 1000-2500m, reaching as high as 3000m in the Andes and as low as sea-level in Southwest Ecuador. Their habitat geography ranges from mountainous regions to foothills, but they avoid flat lowlands. .
Smoke-colored Pewees are typically resident; they do not migrate.


Behavior
The smoke-colored pewee is often found perched on small branches at middle elevations. This differentiates it from the closely related Blackish pewee, which is slightly smaller and perches closer to the ground.


Vocalization
The smoke-colored pewee has a range of calls, with the most common being a loud "pip pip pip". At dawn a "where-di-WIT… whew" can be heard. A clear, repeated "peeew" whistle is also common. Though rare, alternate calls range from a hoarse, repeated "zur" to a whistled "whueer" to short "pjeek pjeek" notes.


Breeding and reproduction
Smoke-colored pewees produce altricial chicks, meaning they require a significant amount of parental care. They create cup-shaped nests made of moss and lichen in high, horizontal branches. White eggs are flecked with brown and lavender. One study found egg incubation period to be 16 days, with the nestling leaving the nest 21 days after hatching. Both male and female parent participated in feeding the chick, while only one bird constructed the nest.
Of nine nests studied, all were active in the drier months of September to December. The mean nest height was 8.8m, eggs are covered an average of 67% of daylight hours, and nestlings received around 3-10 feedings per hour. Clutch ranges from 2-4 eggs.


Diet
The smoke-colored pewee primarily consumes insects that they obtain through sallying. Sallying refers to a foraging technique commonly used by flycatchers, where a bird will catch insects from the air but return to a perch to feed. Birds often return to the same perch between each feeding session.


Conservation and threats
The smoke-colored species is categorized as a species of least concern. The bird can be found in almost every protected reserve of the Andean range. Its habitat range is large and spread out throughout South America, and the species is generally tolerant of habitat disturbance. The smoke-colored pewee is categorized as fairly common to common.


References


External links

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Smoke-colored_Pewee_-_Colombia_S4E3923_(16685722348).jpg. Francesco Veronesi from Italy, via Wikimedia Commons

[[File:Contopus fumigatus Pibí oscuro Smoke-colored Pewee (juveniles) (13975635296).jpg|thumb|Contopus fumigatus, Smoke-colored Pewee (juveniles) perched on wire]]

[[File:Smoke-colored PeWee.jpg|thumb|Smoke-colored pewee displays its slight crest and grey coloration]]

[[File:Smoke-colored Pewee 2015-06-04 (1) (39598674034).jpg|thumb|Smoke-colored Pewee perches on a small twig, displaying its size. 2015-06-04. Alambi Cloud Forest Lodge, Tandayapa, Pichincha Province, Ecuador]]

The '''smoke-colored pewee''' ('''''Contopus fumigatus''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Tyrannidae]]. The species is characterized by a uniform dusky-grey [[plumage]].

Smoke-colored pewees are found in [[Argentina]], [[Bolivia]], [[Brazil]], [[Colombia]], [[Ecuador]], [[Guyana]], [[Peru]], and [[Venezuela]]. Their natural [[habitat]]s are subtropical or tropical dry [[forest]]s, subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]]s, and heavily degraded former forests.

== Taxonomy and systematics ==
The smoke-colored pewee belongs to the family Tyrannidae, known as the [[tyrant flycatcher]]s.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|title=Smoke colored Pewee (Contopus fumigatus)|url= https://www.peruaves.org/tyrannidae/smoke-colored-pewee-contopus-fumigatus|access-date=2021-11-11|website=www.peruaves.org/}}</ref> The Tyrannidae are considered to be the largest family of birds on earth, containing over 400 species. The group shows large variation in form and plumage and can be found in every country in the [[Americas]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Tyrant flycatchers « IOC World Bird List|url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/flycatchers/|access-date=2020-10-02|website=www.worldbirdnames.org|archive-date=2020-04-24|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200424141908/https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/flycatchers/|url-status=live}}</ref>

The species was first formally described by the French naturalists [[Alcide d'Orbigny]] and [[Frédéric de Lafresnaye]] in 1837, based on a specimen found in the [[Bolivian Yungas]]. The [[Generic name (biology)|generic name]] ''Contopus'' derives from the [[Ancient Greek]] ''kontos'' (pole) and ''pous'' (foot). The [[Specific name (zoology)|specific name]] ''fumigatus'' comes from the Latin ''fumus'' (smoke).<ref>{{cite journal |last1=d'Orbigny |first1=Alcide |last2=de Lafresnaye |first2=Frédéric |title=Synopsis Avium |journal=Magasin de Zoologie |volume=Septième Année |issue=CL II |page=43 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/37087488#page/159/mode/1up |access-date=16 November 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Jobling |first1=James A. |title=The Helm dictionary of scientific bird names: from aalge to zusii |date=2010 |publisher=Christopher Helm |location=London |isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 |pages=117, 166}}</ref>

Six [[subspecies]] are recognized:

* ''C. f. ardosiacus'' ([[Frédéric de Lafresnaye|Lafresnaye]], 1844) - Colombia and W Venezuela to E Ecuador and NE Peru
* ''C. f. cineraceus'' ([[Frédéric de Lafresnaye|Lafresnaye]], 1848) - Subtropical N Venezuela (Yaracuy to Miranda)
* ''C. f. duidae'' ([[Frank Chapman (ornithologist)|Chapman]], 1929) - Tepuis of S Venezuela (s Bolívar and Amazonas), adj. Guyana
* ''C. f. zarumae'' ([[Frank Chapman (ornithologist)|Chapman]], 1924) - SW Colombia (Nariño), W Ecuador, and NW Peru (south to southwestern Cajamarca)
* ''C. f. fumigatus'' - the nominate subspecies ([[Alcide d'Orbigny|d'Orbigny]] & [[Frédéric de Lafresnaye|Lafresnaye]], 1837) - SE Peru (Puno) and W Bolivia (La Paz and Cochabamba)
* ''C. f. brachyrhynchus'' ([[Jean Cabanis|Cabanis]], 1883) SE Peru (Puno) and W Bolivia (La Paz and Cochabamba)<ref>{{cite web |last1=Gill |first1=F. |last2=Donsker |first2=D |last3=Rasmussen |first3=P. (Eds.) |title=Master Lists – IOC World Bird List |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/ioc-lists/master-list-2/ |website=IOC World Bird List |access-date=16 November 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Contopus fumigatus (Smoke-colored Pewee) - Avibase |url=https://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=ECF2FBB2B62909A8 |website=avibase.bsc-eoc.org |access-date=16 November 2021}}</ref>

== Description ==

The smoke-colored pewee is a grey bird with a slight [[Crest (feathers)|crest]] on its head.<ref name=":4">{{Cite web|title=Smoke colored Pewee|url= https://ebird.org/species/smcpew1|access-date=2021-11-11|website=ebird.org/home /}}</ref> Their beaks are orange with a black [[culmen (bird)|culmen]].<ref name=":2" /> Mature birds are 16–17 cm in size and 18-20g in weight.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Smoke colored Pewee|url= https://www.oiseaux.net/birds/smoke-colored.pewee.html|access-date=2021-11-11|website=www.oiseaux.net//}}</ref><ref name=":6">{{Cite web| last1=Farnsworth|first1=A.|last2=Lebbin|first2=D. J.|date=2020-03-04|title=Smoke colored Pewee|url= https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/smcpew1/cur/introduction|access-date=2021-11-11|website=birdsoftheworld.org/bow/home}}</ref> This species displays no [[sexual dimorphism]], meaning both sexes share the same [[plumage]]. Their feet are [[dactyly|anisodactyl]], therefore three digits on their foot are forward and one is back. This is the most common foot type in [[passerine|perching birds]].

===Plumages===

Adult smoke-colored pewees have a uniform grey plumage,<ref name=":2" /> though the upper body and wing tips may be tinged with olive or a pale brown.<ref name=":2" /> The crown is a dark gray with a [[Crest (feathers)|crest]].<ref name=":6" /> The edges of the tertials may be a light brown.<ref name=":6" /> Two pale, narrow wingbars may be present, but near indistinct.<ref name=":6" /> The throat, breast, and belly may be a slightly paler grey.<ref name=":6" /> The undertail-coverts are yellowish-white.<ref name=":6" /> Both sexes look alike.<ref name=":4" /> As you move North in smoke-colored pewee habitat range, birds typically become darker and greyer.<ref name=":6" />

Juvenile smoke-colored pewees are typically lighter and browner than mature birds.<ref name=":6" /> A fringed buff of feathers can be seen at the bird's [[nape]].<ref name=":6" /> Two to three wingbars are visible, and both the wingbars and belly are slightly ochre.<ref name=":6" />

==Habitat and distribution==

The smoke-colored pewee is [[endemic]] to the countries of [[Argentina]], [[Bolivia]], [[Brazil]], [[Colombia]], [[Ecuador]], [[Guyana]], [[Peru]], and [[Venezuela]]. Its natural [[habitat]]s are subtropical or tropical dry [[forest]]s, subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]]s, and heavily degraded former forests.<ref name=":6"/> Birds are typically found at 1000-2500m, reaching as high as 3000m in the [[Andes]] and as low as sea-level in Southwest [[Ecuador]].<ref name=":6"/> Their habitat geography ranges from mountainous regions to foothills, but they avoid flat lowlands. .<ref name=":7">{{Cite book|last1=Athanas|first1=N|last2=Greenfield|first2=P.J.|title=Birds of Western Ecuador|date=2016|chapter=Pewees and Tufted Flycatcher|pages=284–285|doi=10.1515/9781400880706-127|isbn=978-1-4008-8070-6}}</ref>

Smoke-colored Pewees are typically resident; they do not [[Migration (ecology)|migrate]].<ref name=":6"/>

==Behavior==

The smoke-colored pewee is often found perched on small branches at middle elevations.<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":7" /> This differentiates it from the closely related [[Blackish pewee]], which is slightly smaller and perches closer to the ground.<ref name=":2" />

===Vocalization===

The smoke-colored pewee has a range of calls, with the most common being a loud "pip pip pip".<ref name=":6"/> At dawn a "where-di-WIT… whew" can be heard. A clear, repeated "peeew" whistle is also common.<ref name=":6"/> Though rare, alternate calls range from a hoarse, repeated "zur" to a whistled "whueer" to short "pjeek pjeek" notes.<ref name=":6"/>

===Breeding and reproduction===

Smoke-colored pewees produce [[altriciality|altricial]] chicks, meaning they require a significant amount of parental care. They create cup-shaped nests made of moss and lichen in high, horizontal branches.<ref name=":6"/> White eggs are flecked with brown and lavender.<ref name=":6"/> One study found egg incubation period to be 16 days, with the nestling leaving the nest 21 days after hatching.<ref name=":8">{{Cite journal|last1=Dyrcz|first1=A.|last2=Greeney|first2=H.F.|date=2010|title= Breeding ecology of the Smoke-colored Pewee (Contopus fumigatus) in northeastern Ecuador|journal=Ornitología Neotropical|volume=21|pages=489–495}}</ref> Both male and female parent participated in feeding the chick, while only one bird constructed the nest.<ref name=":8"/>

Of nine nests studied, all were active in the drier months of September to December.<ref name=":8"/> The mean nest height was 8.8m, eggs are covered an average of 67% of daylight hours, and nestlings received around 3-10 feedings per hour.<ref name=":8"/> [[Clutch (eggs)|Clutch]] ranges from 2-4 eggs.<ref name=":8"/><ref name=":9">{{Cite journal|last1=Heming|first1=N. M.|last2=Greeney|first2=H.F.|last3=Marini|first3=M.A.|date=2020|title= Breeding Biology Research and Data Availability for New World Flycatchers|journal=Natureza & Conservação|volume=11|pages=54–58|doi=10.4322/natcon.2013.009 }}</ref>

===Diet===

The smoke-colored pewee primarily consumes insects that they obtain through sallying.<ref name=":4" /> Sallying refers to a foraging technique commonly used by flycatchers, where a bird will catch insects from the air but return to a perch to feed. Birds often return to the same perch between each feeding session.

==Conservation and threats==

The smoke-colored species is categorized as a species of [[least-concern species|least concern]].<ref name=":4" /> The bird can be found in almost every protected reserve of the Andean range.<ref name=":6"/> Its habitat range is large and spread out throughout [[South America]], and the species is generally tolerant of habitat disturbance.<ref name=":6"/> The smoke-colored pewee is categorized as fairly common to common.<ref name=":6"/>
